title: Health psychology: health problems
summary: The 'health problems' content of the health psychology option asks you to explain ONE health problem — stress or addiction — from more than one perspective, using research. This lesson works stress in depth (the HPA axis and cortisol; Lazarus and Folkman's transactional appraisal model; social factors such as job control), shows how the same demands apply to addiction (the dopamine reward pathway, cognitive expectancy, and social learning), and ends with a full Paper 2 ERQ marked line-by-line against the real 22-mark essay markband.
learningObjectives: Explain one health problem (stress or addiction) using biological, cognitive and sociocultural explanations.
Describe the physiology of the stress response (SAM and HPA axes; adrenaline and cortisol) and the transactional model of appraisal.
Outline biological (dopamine reward), cognitive and social-learning explanations of addiction.
Use named studies (aim, procedure, findings) as SUPPORT for an explanation rather than describing them in isolation.
Evaluate explanations of a health problem and structure a 22-mark ERQ to reach the top markbands.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Why the Body Breaks Down Under Pressure
simpleExplanation.summary: A health problem like stress or addiction is never just 'a body thing'. Biology sets up the machinery (hormones, dopamine), the mind decides how to read the situation (appraisal, expectancy), and the social world supplies the triggers (a low-control job, drug-using peers). A good essay explains one problem through more than one of these lenses and backs each with a study.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of stress like a car alarm. Biology is the alarm system wired into the car (the HPA axis) — useful in an emergency, damaging if it blares for months. Cognition is the sensitivity dial: two identical cars can have the alarm set to go off at a gentle knock or only at a real break-in — that dial is your appraisal of the threat. The social world is the neighbourhood: park on a rough street (a low-control, high-demand job) and the alarm is triggered far more often. To explain why one car's battery keeps dying, you need all three.
steps: Choose ONE health problem (stress or addiction) and stick to it across the whole essay.
Explain it from at least two perspectives — e.g. biological (HPA/cortisol) and cognitive (appraisal).
For each explanation, USE one study: state the finding and link it straight back to the explanation and the question.
Evaluate — reductionism, correlation vs causation, and how the perspectives combine in the biopsychosocial model — then conclude.

flashcards.back: A physical or mental condition — the option names stress, obesity, chronic pain and addiction — that psychologists explain using biological, cognitive and sociocultural factors. In an essay you usually explain just ONE.

A physiological and psychological response to a perceived demand or threat (a stressor). Acute (short-term) stress is adaptive; chronic (long-term) stress is linked to cardiovascular disease, immune suppression and depression.

The sympathetic-adrenal-medullary axis: the FAST 'fight or flight' pathway. The sympathetic nervous system triggers the adrenal medulla to release adrenaline (and noradrenaline), raising heart rate and mobilising energy within seconds.

The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis: the SLOWER, sustained pathway. Hypothalamus releases CRH -> pituitary releases ACTH -> adrenal cortex releases cortisol. Cortisol frees glucose but, when chronically elevated, harms memory, immunity and the cardiovascular system.

The primary human stress hormone, the end product of the HPA axis. Short-term it is adaptive (energy); chronically high cortisol is associated with hippocampal damage, impaired memory and suppressed immune function.

Stress arises from the 'transaction' between a person and their environment. PRIMARY appraisal asks 'Is this a threat?'; SECONDARY appraisal asks 'Can I cope?'. Stress results when perceived demands exceed perceived resources — so it is the interpretation, not the event, that stresses us.

Lower socioeconomic status and, crucially, lower JOB CONTROL are associated with higher chronic stress and stress-related illness. A social factor, not a purely individual one — evidenced by the Whitehall studies of British civil servants.
A chronic, relapsing condition marked by compulsive drug seeking/use (or a behaviour) despite harmful consequences, plus tolerance and withdrawal. Explained biologically, cognitively and socially.

The mesolimbic pathway (ventral tegmental area -> nucleus accumbens). Addictive drugs sharply raise dopamine here, reinforcing use. Repeated overstimulation is linked to reduced dopamine D2 receptor availability, so more of the drug is needed for the same reward (tolerance).

Bandura's social learning theory: people acquire drug use by observing and imitating models (peers, family, media) and by vicarious reinforcement — seeing a model rewarded (status, belonging) makes imitation more likely.
Adrenaline is the FAST SAM response; cortisol is the SLOWER HPA end product. Writing 'the HPA axis releases adrenaline' is a classic error that undermines Criterion B (knowledge).

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: describing vs USING a study
flashcards.back: Reciting a study's method is DESCRIBING it. USING it means stating the finding and linking it explicitly to the explanation and the question. Under our markband, described-but-not-used research is capped in Criterion C.

quickCheck.prompt: During PROLONGED, chronic stress, the hormone released as the end product of the relevant axis is:
options: Adrenaline, from the SAM axis
Cortisol, from the HPA axis
Dopamine, from the mesolimbic pathway
Noradrenaline, from the pituitary gland

quickCheck.prompt: The immediate 'fight or flight' surge in heart rate a few seconds after a sudden threat is driven mainly by:
options: The HPA axis releasing cortisol
The SAM axis releasing adrenaline
The adrenal cortex releasing ACTH
Reduced dopamine D2 receptor availability

quickCheck.prompt: In Lazarus and Folkman's transactional model, SECONDARY appraisal is the evaluation of:
options: Whether the situation is a threat
Whether one has the resources to cope with it
How much adrenaline is being released
How much social support society provides
quickCheck.answer: Whether one has the resources to cope with it

quickCheck.prompt: The biological reward explanation argues that addictive drugs are reinforcing mainly because they:
options: Lower cortisol output from the HPA axis
Sharply raise dopamine in the mesolimbic reward pathway
Increase leptin so the person feels satisfied
Reduce activity in the sympathetic nervous system
quickCheck.answer: Sharply raise dopamine in the mesolimbic reward pathway

quickCheck.prompt: In a Paper 2 essay, a study counts as being USED (not merely described) when you:
options: Write out its full procedure and exact sample size
List the ethical guidelines it followed
State its finding and link it explicitly to the explanation and the question
Name the researchers and the year of publication
quickCheck.answer: State its finding and link it explicitly to the explanation and the question

faq.q: The question says 'one health problem' — can I write about both stress and addiction?
faq.a: No. Choose one and develop it fully. Splitting an essay across two health problems means neither gets the depth of knowledge (Criterion B) or use of research (Criterion C) needed for the top bands, and it usually blurs your focus (Criterion A). This lesson teaches stress in depth and gives addiction as the alternative choice — pick whichever you can support with two solid studies.

faq.q: How many explanations and studies do I actually need for a 22-mark essay?
faq.a: Two well-developed explanations, each supported by a study that you USE, is the reliable route to the top bands — quality over quantity. For stress, a strong pairing is the biological HPA/cortisol explanation and the cognitive appraisal explanation. Three thinly-covered explanations score worse than two developed ones, because Criteria B, C and D all reward depth and explicit linking, not a list.

faq.q: How much biological detail do I need for the HPA axis?
faq.a: Enough to explain the mechanism accurately: hypothalamus (CRH) -> pituitary (ACTH) -> adrenal cortex (cortisol), and why chronically high cortisol is harmful. You do not need biochemistry. The marks come from linking that mechanism to the health outcome and to the question — accurate mechanism plus explicit link, not memorised jargon.

sections.content: The health psychology option can feel bottomless — stress, obesity, chronic pain, addiction, promotion, prevention. But the 'health problems' content asks a narrow, repeatable thing: take ONE health problem and explain it from more than one perspective, using research. Master that pattern on a single problem and you can meet almost any essay in this section. This lesson works stress in full — the physiology, the appraisal model, and the social gradient — then shows the identical demands applied to addiction, before marking a complete 22-mark essay against the real Paper 2 markband.
How examiners frame 'health problems'

Health psychology rests on the biopsychosocial model: health and illness emerge from the interaction of biological factors (genes, hormones, neurotransmitters), psychological factors (thoughts, appraisals, expectancies) and social factors (culture, socioeconomic status, relationships). For any single health problem, all three levels are relevant, and a strong essay explains ONE problem through at least two of them rather than listing symptoms.

Watch the command term. 'Explain' or 'Outline' wants the mechanism made clear; 'Discuss' or 'Evaluate' additionally wants critical thinking — strengths, limitations and competing perspectives. Whatever the term, the structural move is the same: choose one problem, commit to it, and support each explanation with a study you actually use.
Health problem in focus: stress

Stress is the body's response to a stressor — any demand perceived as threatening or exceeding one's resources. Acute stress is adaptive: it sharpens attention and mobilises energy to deal with an immediate challenge. The problem is chronic stress. When the response stays switched on for weeks or months, the same systems that once protected us begin to cause harm: chronic stress is associated with hypertension and cardiovascular disease, suppressed immune function, impaired memory, and raised risk of anxiety and depression. Because prolonged activation is so common in modern life, stress is one of the most widely studied — and most examinable — health problems in the option.
Biological explanation: the HPA axis and cortisol

The physiological stress response runs on two pathways. The FAST one is the SAM axis (sympathetic-adrenal-medullary): the sympathetic nervous system signals the adrenal medulla to release adrenaline within seconds, producing the racing heart and heightened alertness of 'fight or flight'. The SLOWER, sustained one is the HPA axis (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal): the hypothalamus releases CRH, which prompts the pituitary to release ACTH, which drives the adrenal cortex to release cortisol. Cortisol frees glucose for prolonged effort — useful in the short term. But when cortisol stays elevated for months, it is implicated in damage to the hippocampus (harming memory), suppression of the immune system, and cardiovascular strain. This is the mechanism by which a psychological experience becomes a physical illness.

items: **SAM axis — fast:** sympathetic nervous system -> adrenal medulla -> ADRENALINE. Seconds. Racing heart, 'fight or flight'.
**HPA axis — slow, sustained:** hypothalamus (CRH) -> pituitary (ACTH) -> adrenal cortex -> CORTISOL. Minutes to hours; can stay elevated for months.
**Chronic cortisol harms:** hippocampal/memory damage, immune suppression, raised cardiovascular risk — the bridge from psychological stress to physical illness.
**Key discrimination:** adrenaline is SAM; cortisol is HPA. Mixing them up is a recurring examiner-penalised error.

sections.content: Supporting study — Newcomer et al. (1999). Aim: to test whether cortisol at the levels produced during stress impairs memory. Procedure: healthy adults were given tablets over several days — a high dose of cortisol equivalent to a major stressor, a low dose, or a placebo — and were tested on verbal declarative memory (recalling prose). Findings: the high-cortisol group showed significantly worse recall than the low-dose and placebo groups, and the impairment reversed once the cortisol was stopped. USING this study: it provides experimental (not merely correlational) support for the HPA explanation — manipulating cortisol directly changed cognitive performance — which is why we can argue cortisol is a cause of stress's harmful effects on memory, not just a correlate.
Cognitive explanation: appraisal (Lazarus & Folkman)

The biological account explains the machinery but not why the same event floors one person and energises another. Lazarus and Folkman's transactional model (1984) fills that gap. Stress, they argue, arises from the transaction between the person and the situation, mediated by two appraisals. PRIMARY appraisal asks 'Is this a threat, a challenge, or irrelevant?'. SECONDARY appraisal asks 'Do I have the resources to cope?'. Stress results only when perceived demands exceed perceived coping resources. On this view it is not the event itself but its interpretation that triggers the stress response — which is precisely why the model can explain individual differences that biology alone cannot.

Supporting study — Speisman et al. (1964). Aim: to test whether manipulating cognitive appraisal changes the physiological stress response to the SAME stimulus. Procedure: participants watched a distressing film of a painful rite while hearing one of several soundtracks — a 'trauma' track emphasising the pain, a 'denial' track downplaying it, an 'intellectualisation' track giving a detached anthropological narration, or silence. Physiological arousal (heart rate, galvanic skin response) was recorded. Findings: arousal was highest with the trauma track and markedly lower with the denial and intellectualisation tracks, even though the images were identical. USING this study: because only the appraisal — not the stimulus — was varied, the result directly supports the claim that appraisal shapes the stress response, giving the cognitive explanation experimental grounding.
Social factors in stress

Stress is not only inside the body and mind; social conditions shape how often the response fires and how long it lasts. Chronic stressors cluster by socioeconomic status: financial insecurity, poor housing, discrimination and, in particular, low control over one's work. The Whitehall studies of British civil servants (Marmot and colleagues) found a striking social gradient: lower employment grades had substantially higher rates of stress-related cardiovascular disease, and this was linked especially to low job control — how much say a person had over their own work — rather than to income alone. USING this: it shows a specifiable social variable (job control) predicts a physical health outcome, evidencing the sociocultural explanation and demonstrating that stress is patterned by social structure, not just individual temperament.

sections.question: Short-answer / SAQ practice: Explain one biological explanation of stress. [9]
sections.solution:
**Point:** One biological explanation locates the harm of stress in prolonged activation of the HPA axis and the hormone cortisol.

**Mechanism (knowledge):** Under a sustained stressor, the hypothalamus releases CRH, prompting the pituitary to release ACTH, which drives the adrenal cortex to release cortisol. Cortisol mobilises glucose for prolonged effort — adaptive in the short term. But when cortisol stays elevated for weeks or months it is implicated in hippocampal damage, immune suppression and cardiovascular strain, converting a psychological experience into physical illness.

**Evidence (USED, not just described):** Newcomer et al. (1999) gave healthy adults a high (stress-level) dose of cortisol, a low dose, or placebo, and tested verbal memory. The high-cortisol group recalled significantly less prose, and the deficit reversed when cortisol stopped. Because cortisol was *manipulated*, this is experimental support that cortisol *causes* stress-related memory impairment, not merely correlates with it — which is why the study strengthens the biological explanation rather than just illustrating it.

**Brief evaluation:** The explanation is well supported and mechanistic, but reductionist — it cannot explain why the same stressor produces very different responses across individuals, which the cognitive appraisal model addresses. A complete account is biopsychosocial.

sections.content: In SAQs and ERQs alike, the difference between a middle and a top answer is the sentence AFTER the study. Do not stop at 'Newcomer found high cortisol impaired memory'. Add the link: 'because cortisol was manipulated, this shows cortisol causes the impairment, supporting the HPA explanation.' That linking sentence is what the marking engine reads as USE of research.
The alternative: explaining addiction

If you prefer addiction as your 'one health problem', the demands are identical — explain it from more than one perspective, using research. Addiction is a chronic, relapsing condition of compulsive drug use (or behaviour) despite harm, with tolerance and withdrawal. It carries heavy effects — health damage, social and economic cost — and high relapse rates, which is exactly why single-perspective explanations fall short.

items: **Biological — dopamine reward:** addictive drugs sharply raise dopamine in the mesolimbic pathway (VTA -> nucleus accumbens), powerfully reinforcing use. Volkow et al.'s neuroimaging work links addiction to reduced dopamine D2 receptor availability, so more drug is needed for the same reward (tolerance) — a mechanism for compulsive use. USE it to argue addiction has a neurobiological basis, not just weak willpower.

**Cognitive — expectancy and attentional bias:** users hold outcome expectancies ('this will relax me / help me cope') that drive use, and develop an attentional bias toward drug cues. Field and Cox's review of attentional-bias research shows addicted individuals' attention is automatically captured by drug-related stimuli, feeding craving and relapse — evidence for a cognitive maintenance mechanism.

**Sociocultural — social learning:** Bandura's social learning theory explains initiation through observation and imitation of models (peers, family, media) and vicarious reinforcement — seeing use rewarded with status or belonging. Consistent findings that parental and peer substance use predict adolescent uptake support a social pathway into addiction.

sections.question: ERQ practice: Explain one biological explanation of addiction, using research. [Discuss-style paragraph]
sections.solution:
**Claim:** A leading biological explanation is that addiction hijacks the brain's dopamine reward system.

**Mechanism:** Addictive drugs cause a large, rapid rise in dopamine in the mesolimbic pathway (ventral tegmental area to nucleus accumbens), the circuit that normally reinforces survival behaviours like eating. This flood of dopamine strongly reinforces drug-taking. With repeated use the system adapts — dopamine D2 receptor availability falls — so ordinary rewards feel flat and larger drug doses are needed for the same effect (tolerance), driving escalating, compulsive use.

**Evidence (USED):** Volkow et al. used brain imaging to compare drug-dependent and control participants and found significantly reduced striatal dopamine D2 receptor availability in the addicted group. Linking back: this supports the reward explanation because it identifies a specific neural adaptation that would make natural rewards less satisfying and the drug more compelling — a physical basis for compulsion, answering the question of *why* users continue despite harm.

**Evaluation:** The account is strong on the physiology of maintenance but reductionist: it under-explains why exposed individuals differ in whether they become addicted, and much human data are correlational — reduced D2 receptors may partly precede, not only follow, drug use. Combining it with cognitive (expectancy) and social-learning explanations gives the fuller, biopsychosocial picture examiners reward.

sections.content: Evaluating explanations of health problems

items: **Reductionism vs holism:** a purely biological account (cortisol, dopamine) explains mechanism but ignores meaning and context; a purely social account ignores why exposed individuals differ. The biopsychosocial model integrates them — say so explicitly, and use one perspective to critique another.

**Correlation vs causation:** much health-psychology evidence is correlational (e.g. Whitehall's link between job control and heart disease). Prefer 'is associated with' / 'is a risk factor for'. Praise experimental studies (Newcomer; Speisman) precisely because manipulation licenses causal claims.

**Individual and cultural differences:** appraisal and coping vary across people and cultures, and much research (including the Whitehall sample) is drawn from specific populations — so limits generalisability.

**Ecological validity vs control:** lab studies (Speisman's film) gain control but may not capture real chronic stress; field/epidemiological studies (Whitehall) gain realism but lose control. Use this trade-off to weigh evidence, not just list it.
**Bidirectionality:** stress and health, or drug use and D2 receptors, may influence each other in both directions — a mature point that lifts Criterion D.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing SAM and HPA:** writing that the HPA axis releases adrenaline, or that cortisol is the fast 'fight or flight' hormone. Adrenaline = SAM (fast); cortisol = HPA (slow). This directly costs Criterion B.

**Describing studies instead of USING them:** narrating Newcomer's or Speisman's procedure without the sentence that links the finding to the explanation and the question. Under our markband, described-but-not-used research is capped in Criterion C.
**Answering on two health problems:** the question says 'one'. Covering both stress and addiction sacrifices the depth (Criterion B) and focus (Criterion A) the top bands require.
**Treating appraisal as biological, or the HPA axis as cognitive:** keep the perspectives straight — appraisal is a COGNITIVE process; the HPA axis is a BIOLOGICAL mechanism.
**Claiming causation from correlational data:** e.g. asserting low job control 'causes' heart disease from Whitehall alone. Use cautious language and reserve causal claims for experiments.
**Evaluation bolted on as a list:** strengths/limitations dumped in a final paragraph with no link to the argument. Weave critical thinking through the essay for Criterion D.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 2 markband

sections.question: Paper 2 ERQ: Discuss explanations for one health problem (e.g. stress or addiction). [22]
sections.solution:
**Model essay (health problem: stress)**

**Thesis / focus:** Stress — the response to a demand perceived as exceeding one's resources — becomes a serious health problem only when it is chronic, and no single perspective explains it fully. This essay argues that stress is best understood biopsychosocially, developing a biological explanation (the HPA axis and cortisol) and a cognitive explanation (Lazarus and Folkman's appraisal model), and using each perspective to critique the other before reaching an integrated conclusion.

**Explanation 1 — Biological (HPA/cortisol):** The physiological response runs on two pathways. The fast SAM axis releases adrenaline for immediate 'fight or flight'. The slower HPA axis sustains the response: the hypothalamus releases CRH, prompting the pituitary to release ACTH, which drives the adrenal cortex to release cortisol. Cortisol is adaptive briefly — mobilising glucose — but chronically elevated cortisol is implicated in hippocampal damage, immune suppression and cardiovascular disease. This is the mechanism by which prolonged psychological stress produces physical illness.

*Research used:* Newcomer et al. (1999) gave healthy participants a high (stress-level) dose of cortisol, a low dose, or placebo, and tested verbal declarative memory. The high-cortisol group recalled significantly less, and the deficit reversed when cortisol stopped. Because cortisol was experimentally *manipulated*, this supports the causal claim that cortisol impairs cognition under stress — not merely that the two co-occur — giving the biological explanation firm, experimental grounding.

**Explanation 2 — Cognitive (appraisal):** Biology explains the machinery but not why identical events stress people differently. Lazarus and Folkman's transactional model holds that stress arises from the transaction between person and situation via two appraisals: primary ('Is this a threat?') and secondary ('Can I cope?'). Stress results only when perceived demands exceed perceived resources — so interpretation, not the event itself, is decisive, which lets the model explain individual differences biology cannot.

*Research used:* Speisman et al. (1964) showed participants the same distressing film with different soundtracks manipulating appraisal — 'trauma' (emphasising pain) versus 'denial' and 'intellectualisation' (detached). Physiological arousal was highest for the trauma track and markedly lower for the detached tracks, though the images were identical. Because only appraisal was varied, the study directly supports the claim that appraisal shapes the stress response, grounding the cognitive explanation experimentally.

**Critical thinking (developed):** The two explanations are complementary, and each exposes the other's limits. The biological account is mechanistically strong and, via Newcomer, causally supported, but reductionist — it cannot say why one person's HPA axis fires at a stressor another shrugs off. The appraisal model supplies that missing variable, yet it is incomplete alone: appraisals eventually act *through* the HPA axis, so cognition and physiology are not rivals but stages of one process. Both lab studies trade ecological validity for control — Speisman's brief film is far from months of real chronic stress — which is why field evidence matters: the Whitehall studies found lower job control predicts stress-related cardiovascular disease, adding a social layer but only correlationally, so causal claims there stay cautious. Read together, the strongest position is the biopsychosocial model, in which social conditions set the frequency of stressors, appraisal determines whether they are threatening, and the HPA axis delivers the physical toll.

**Conclusion:** Explanations of stress are most convincing when combined. The HPA/cortisol account, supported experimentally by Newcomer, explains how stress harms the body; the appraisal model, supported by Speisman, explains why the same event harms people unequally; and social evidence such as Whitehall shows the pattern is structured, not random. Because each perspective answers a question the others cannot, stress is best explained biopsychosocially rather than by any one factor alone.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22):**

- **A — Focus on the question (2/2):** The introduction defines stress, restates the command 'discuss', and commits to ONE health problem with a clear thesis; every paragraph serves that thesis. Top band because the demands of the question are understood and sustained throughout.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6):** Two explanations are developed with accurate detail — the correct CRH -> ACTH -> cortisol sequence, the SAM/HPA distinction, and the primary/secondary appraisal structure — and psychological terminology is used precisely. Top band: knowledge is accurate, detailed and relevant, not a list.
- **C — Use of research (6/6):** Newcomer and Speisman are not just described; each finding is linked back to its explanation and to the question, and their experimental design is used to justify causal claims. Whitehall is added and correctly flagged as correlational. Top band because research is explicitly USED to support the argument.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6):** Evaluation is woven through, not bolted on: reductionism, the biological/cognitive integration, correlation vs causation (Whitehall), and the ecological-validity/control trade-off, all serving the biopsychosocial thesis. Top band: critical thinking is developed and organised around a clear line of argument.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2):** A logical thesis -> explanations -> integrated evaluation -> conclusion structure, with clear signposting. Top band.

**Total: 22/22.** The single move that pushes this to the top of every criterion is the same one: after each study, a sentence that USES the finding to advance the argument — the difference between description (mid-band) and analysis (top band).

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this [22]-mark question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official Paper 2 markband, criterion by criterion, telling you which band you hit and why. Two caps decide most essays: (1) research that is DESCRIBED but not USED cannot rise into the top band of Criterion C (Use of research) — you must link each finding to the argument; and (2) an essay that is mainly DESCRIPTIVE, with evaluation absent or merely listed, is held in the bottom band of Criterion D (Critical thinking). Clear those two caps and the top bands open up.
Common to SL and HL

The health psychology option, including the 'health problems' content, is common to SL and HL at this level — the explanations, studies and 22-mark Paper 2 essay demands are the same for both. There is no separate HL extension for this material, so the model answer and markband above apply to every student taking this option.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 essay marked: discuss explanations for one health problem (stress or addiction) [22]

items.label: National Institute on Drug Abuse — Drugs, Brains, and Behavior: The Science of Addiction
